era_context: The mid-18th century was an era of imperial competition, Enlightenment intellectual ferment, and shifting global power structures. In Europe, the post-War of Austrian Succession peace was fragile, with great powers maneuvering toward the alliances that would produce the Seven Years' War (1756–1763). Britain, France, and Spain competed for colonial dominance in the Americas and India. The Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor governed a vast and prosperous China, while the Mughal Empire was fragmenting into successor states and the Afghan Durrani Empire was expanding aggressively. In Southeast Asia, the Toungoo dynasty collapsed and a new Burmese unification began.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (interior polities such as Lunda, Lozi, and Great Lakes kingdoms poorly documented in European sources)
Central Asia (Kazakh and Uzbek khanate records underrepresented in English-language historiography)
Indigenous peoples of the Americas beyond European contact zones

events.title: Britain adopts the Gregorian calendar
events.description: Under the Calendar (New Style) Act 1750, Britain and its colonies implemented the Gregorian calendar in September 1752. Wednesday 2 September was followed by Thursday 14 September, skipping eleven days to align with the Gregorian system already used by most of Catholic Europe since 1582. Additionally, the legal start of the year had already shifted from 25 March to 1 January on 1 January 1752. The reform affected financial calculations, rents, legal dates, and religious observances across the entire British Empire, including the American colonies.
key_figures: Philip Dormer Stanhope, 4th Earl of Chesterfield (chief parliamentary advocate)
George Parker, 2nd Earl of Macclesfield (astronomer and parliamentary supporter)
James Bradley (Astronomer Royal, provided technical guidance)

sources.name: Calendar (New Style) Act 1750 (24 Geo. II c.23), Parliamentary statute text
Robert Poole, Time's Alteration: Calendar Reform in Early Modern England (1998)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary statute survives; implementation dates are undisputed.

events.title: Benjamin Franklin's kite experiment demonstrates electrical nature of lightning
events.description: In June 1752, Benjamin Franklin reportedly conducted his famous kite experiment in Philadelphia, flying a kite in a thunderstorm to demonstrate that lightning is electrical in nature. He drew sparks from a key attached to the kite string to a Leyden jar, confirming his hypothesis. The experiment was described in the Pennsylvania Gazette in October 1752 and in a letter to Peter Collinson read to the Royal Society. The work built on Franklin's earlier theoretical proposals and Thomas-François Dalibard's successful sentry-box experiment in France (May 1752), which had already validated Franklin's hypothesis before the kite flight.
key_figures: Benjamin Franklin (natural philosopher and colonial statesman)
Thomas-François Dalibard (French scientist who first verified Franklin's hypothesis)
Peter Collinson (London correspondent and Fellow of the Royal Society)

sources.name: Benjamin Franklin, letter to Peter Collinson (19 October 1752), published in the Pennsylvania Gazette and later in Experiments and Observations on Electricity (1753 edition)
I. Bernard Cohen, Benjamin Franklin's Science (1990)

events.certainty_note: Franklin's own account was written months after the alleged event; no independent witnesses are documented. Some historians (notably Tom Tucker) have questioned whether the kite experiment occurred as described, though Franklin's broader electrical research is undisputed.

events.title: Fall of Ava and collapse of the Toungoo dynasty in Burma
events.description: In March–April 1752, Mon forces from the restored Hanthawaddy Kingdom captured the Burmese capital of Ava (Inwa), ending the Restored Toungoo dynasty that had ruled Upper Burma since 1597. The last Toungoo king, Mahadhammaraza Dipadi, was taken prisoner. This conquest fragmented Burma politically but immediately provoked a Bamar resistance movement led by Alaungpaya, the chief of Shwebo village, who rallied local forces and began the military campaigns that would found the Konbaung dynasty — the last Burmese royal dynasty.
key_figures: Mahadhammaraza Dipadi (last Toungoo king)
Binnya Dala (Mon military commander)
Alaungpaya (Shwebo village chief, founder of Konbaung dynasty)

sources.name: Hmannan Yazawin (Glass Palace Chronicle), compiled 1829 from earlier court records
Victor Lieberman, Strange Parallels: Southeast Asia in Global Context, c. 800–1830, Vol. 1 (2003)

events.certainty_note: Multiple Burmese and Mon chronicles corroborate the fall of Ava and rise of Alaungpaya; European sources (French and Dutch trading post records) provide independent confirmation.

events.title: Alaungpaya begins resistance and founding of the Konbaung dynasty
events.description: Following the Mon capture of Ava, Alaungpaya (born Aung Zeya), headman of the village of Shwebo (Moksobo), refused to submit to Mon authority and rallied Bamar resistance in Upper Burma. By late 1752, he had consolidated control over the Shwebo district and began expanding, defeating Mon garrisons and attracting followers. His movement would ultimately reunify Burma under the Konbaung dynasty by 1757, creating a state that would endure until the final British annexation in 1885.
key_figures: Alaungpaya (resistance leader and dynasty founder)

sources.name: Hmannan Yazawin (Glass Palace Chronicle)
G.E. Harvey, History of Burma (1925)

events.certainty_note: Well attested in multiple chronicle traditions; timeline confirmed by European trading post records.

events.title: Ahmad Shah Durrani's fourth invasion of India; Mughal cession of Punjab and Sindh
events.description: Ahmad Shah Durrani launched his fourth invasion of the Indian subcontinent in 1752, capturing Lahore and reasserting Afghan control over the Punjab. The weakened Mughal court, unable to mount effective resistance, formally ceded the provinces of Punjab, Sindh, and parts of Kashmir to the Durrani Empire. This cession marked a major step in the disintegration of effective Mughal sovereignty and established the Durrani Empire as a major power straddling Central and South Asia. The invasion further destabilized northern India and set the stage for future Afghan-Maratha conflict.
key_figures: Ahmad Shah Durrani (founder of the Durrani Empire)
Ahmad Shah Bahadur (Mughal Emperor, r. 1748–1754)
Mir Mannu (Mughal governor of Punjab, submitted to Durrani overlordship)

sources.name: Tarikh-i Ahmad Shahi by Mahmud al-Husayni (contemporary chronicle of Ahmad Shah's reign)
Jos J.L. Gommans, The Rise of the Indo-Afghan Empire: c. 1710–1780 (1995)

events.certainty_note: Multiple Persian-language chronicles and Mughal court records confirm the invasion and cession. Exact terms of the cession are debated in detail but the broad event is undisputed.

events.title: Treaty of Aranjuez between Spain and Austria
events.description: On 14 June 1752, Spain and Austria signed the Treaty of Aranjuez, settling outstanding Italian territorial disputes from the War of Austrian Succession. Spain recognized Austrian control of the Duchy of Milan and other Italian possessions, while Austria guaranteed the succession of the Spanish Bourbons in the Duchy of Parma and the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies. The treaty stabilized the Italian peninsula diplomatically and represented a broader normalization of Bourbon-Habsburg relations, prefiguring the later Family Compact and Diplomatic Revolution.
key_figures: Ferdinand VI (King of Spain)
Maria Theresa (Empress, Habsburg ruler)
José de Carvajal y Lancaster (Spanish Secretary of State)

sources.name: Treaty text in Cantillo, Tratados, convenios y declaraciones de paz y de comercio (1843), compiling original diplomatic documents
John Lynch, Bourbon Spain, 1700–1808 (1989)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in Spanish and Austrian diplomatic archives.

events.title: David Hume publishes Political Discourses
events.description: In 1752, the Scottish philosopher David Hume published Political Discourses in Edinburgh, a collection of essays on economic and political subjects including trade, money, interest, taxation, and population. The work was immediately influential and widely translated, becoming arguably Hume's most commercially successful publication during his lifetime. The essays significantly influenced Adam Smith, the French Physiocrats, and the broader development of political economy as a discipline. Hume's arguments for free trade and against mercantilist bullionism were particularly impactful.
key_figures: David Hume (philosopher and essayist)

sources.name: David Hume, Political Discourses (Edinburgh: R. Fleming for A. Kincaid and A. Donaldson, 1752), first edition
Ernest Campbell Mossner, The Life of David Hume (1954; 2nd ed. 1980)

events.certainty_note: First edition copies survive; publication date is undisputed.

events.title: Murder Act passed in Britain
events.description: The Murder Act 1752 (25 Geo. II c.37) was enacted by the British Parliament, adding post-mortem punishments for convicted murderers: judges could order the body to be publicly dissected by surgeons or hung in chains (gibbeted). The Act was intended to add 'further terror and peculiar mark of infamy' to the punishment of murder. It had the secondary effect of providing a legal supply of cadavers for anatomical study, partially addressing the chronic shortage that fueled the body-snatching trade, though it did not eliminate the practice.
key_figures: Henry Pelham (Prime Minister)
William Lee (Lord Chief Justice)

sources.name: Murder Act 1752, 25 Geo. II c.37, Parliamentary statute text
Peter Linebaugh, The London Hanged: Crime and Civil Society in the Eighteenth Century (1991)

events.certainty_note: Statute text survives; passage date is undisputed.

events.title: Great Hurricane strikes Charleston, South Carolina
events.description: On 15 September 1752 (New Style), a major hurricane struck Charleston, South Carolina, causing severe destruction. The storm surge flooded the city, destroying wharves, warehouses, and ships in the harbor. Contemporary accounts describe winds stripping buildings to their foundations and a storm tide rising over ten feet. The hurricane caused significant loss of life and devastated the rice and indigo plantations that formed the economic backbone of the colony. It was one of the most destructive hurricanes in colonial American history.
key_figures: James Glen (Royal Governor of South Carolina)

sources.name: South Carolina Gazette accounts (September–October 1752); Governor Glen's correspondence to the Board of Trade
Matthew Mulcahy, Hurricanes and Society in the British Greater Caribbean, 1624–1783 (2006)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary newspaper and government reports survive. Date is affected by the calendar change (occurred days after the Gregorian adoption).

events.title: Gin Act 1751 takes effect, reducing gin consumption in London
events.description: The Gin Act of 1751 (24 Geo. II c.40), which came into full effect in 1752, proved the most successful of several parliamentary attempts to curb the gin epidemic that had devastated London's poor since the 1720s. Unlike earlier failed measures, this act worked with the grain trade by prohibiting distillers from selling directly to the public and requiring retailers to hold licenses from magistrates. Combined with rising grain prices that made gin production more expensive, the act contributed to a significant decline in gin consumption. The 'Gin Craze' that had prompted Hogarth's famous prints began to subside.
key_figures: Henry Fielding (magistrate and novelist, advocated gin reform)
William Hogarth (artist, his 1751 prints 'Gin Lane' and 'Beer Street' helped build public support)

sources.name: Gin Act 1751, 24 Geo. II c.40, Parliamentary statute text
Jessica Warner, Craze: Gin and Debauchery in an Age of Reason (2002)

events.certainty_note: Statute survives; effectiveness debated by historians but decline in consumption is documented in excise records.

events.title: French expansion into the Ohio Valley intensifies
events.description: In 1752, French-Canadian forces and their Native American allies struck at British-allied trading posts in the Ohio Country. Most notably, in June, Charles-Michel Mouet de Langlade led a force of Ottawa and Ojibwe warriors in an attack on the Miami town of Pickawillany (near present-day Piqua, Ohio), killing the pro-British chief Memeskia (known to the British as 'Old Briton') and destroying the trading post. This raid reasserted French dominance in the region and demonstrated that Britain could not protect its Native allies, pushing many indigenous groups back toward the French alliance. The escalation was a direct precursor to the French and Indian War.
key_figures: Charles-Michel Mouet de Langlade (French-Canadian officer)
Memeskia / La Demoiselle / Old Briton (Miami chief, killed)
Marquis de Duquesne (Governor-General of New France, arriving 1752)

sources.name: French colonial correspondence (Archives nationales d'outre-mer); reports of the attack in Pennsylvania and Virginia colonial records
Fred Anderson, Crucible of War: The Seven Years' War and the Fate of Empire in British North America (2000)

events.certainty_note: Multiple French and British colonial documents record the raid on Pickawillany; date and details well established.

events.title: Liberty Bell cracks during testing in Philadelphia
events.description: The bell commissioned by the Pennsylvania Provincial Assembly from the Whitechapel Bell Foundry in London arrived in Philadelphia in late August or September 1752. When tested, it cracked on the first stroke of the clapper, likely due to the bell metal being too brittle. Two local metalworkers, John Pass and John Stow, were engaged to melt down and recast the bell, a process completed in 1753. The bell — later known as the Liberty Bell — would become an iconic symbol of American independence, though its symbolic significance was largely a 19th-century construction.
key_figures: John Pass (metalworker who recast the bell)
John Stow (metalworker who recast the bell)
Isaac Norris II (Speaker of the Pennsylvania Assembly, who ordered the bell)

sources.name: Pennsylvania Assembly records; Isaac Norris correspondence regarding the bell's defects
Gary B. Nash, The Liberty Bell (2010)

events.certainty_note: Assembly records and Norris's letters document the bell's arrival and cracking. Exact date of arrival uncertain within a few weeks.

events.title: Karim Khan Zand rises as a major power in post-Afsharid Persia
events.description: By 1752, Karim Khan of the Zand tribe had emerged as one of the principal contenders in the civil wars that followed the collapse of the Afsharid dynasty after Nadir Shah's assassination in 1747. Operating from his base in southern and central Iran, Karim Khan contested power with the Afsharid remnants, the Qajar chief Mohammad Hasan Khan, and other regional warlords. He would eventually prevail by the late 1750s and establish the Zand dynasty, ruling as 'Vakil al-Ra'aya' (Regent of the People) rather than claiming the royal title of shah — a notable political choice reflecting his legitimization strategy.
key_figures: Karim Khan Zand (Zand tribal leader)
Mohammad Hasan Khan Qajar (rival claimant)
Abu'l-Fath Khan (nominal Safavid puppet ruler)

sources.name: Rostam al-Hukama, Rostam al-Tavarikh (late 18th-century Persian chronicle)
John R. Perry, Karim Khan Zand: A History of Iran, 1747–1779 (1979)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Karim Khan's rise is well established, but precise dating of specific events in the Persian civil wars of 1747–1760 is often approximate due to conflicting chronicle accounts.

events.title: Construction of the Winter Palace begins in Saint Petersburg
events.description: In 1752, Empress Elizabeth of Russia commissioned the Italian architect Bartolomeo Francesco Rastrelli to design and build a new Winter Palace in Saint Petersburg, replacing the earlier structure. Construction began in 1754 (with planning and preliminary work from 1752–1753), and the monumental Baroque palace would become the principal residence of the Russian tsars and one of the most iconic buildings in European architecture. The project reflected Elizabeth's ambition to make the Russian court rival Versailles in splendor and affirm Russia's status as a major European power.
key_figures: Elizabeth (Empress of Russia, r. 1741–1762)
Bartolomeo Francesco Rastrelli (court architect)

sources.name: Imperial court records and Rastrelli's architectural plans (Hermitage Museum archives)
Dimitri Shvidkovsky, Russian Architecture and the West (2007)

events.certainty_note: Court records document the commission; some sources date the formal construction start to 1754, with Rastrelli's commission and planning work occurring in 1752–1753.

events.title: Marquis de Pombal consolidates power as de facto ruler of Portugal
events.description: Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo (later Marquis of Pombal) continued to consolidate his position as the dominant minister under King Joseph I, who had acceded in 1750. By 1752, Pombal was effectively directing Portuguese domestic and colonial policy, beginning a sweeping program of Enlightened reforms that would transform Portuguese governance, education, and commerce. He moved to centralize state power, curtail noble and clerical privilege, and modernize the Portuguese economy and its vast colonial empire in Brazil, Africa, and Asia.
key_figures: Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo, later Marquis of Pombal (Secretary of State)
Joseph I (King of Portugal, r. 1750–1777)

sources.name: Portuguese royal correspondence and administrative records (Arquivo Nacional da Torre do Tombo)
Kenneth Maxwell, Pombal: Paradox of the Enlightenment (1995)

events.certainty_note: Pombal's rise is well documented in Portuguese state archives, though the precise chronology of his accumulation of power is somewhat gradual rather than marked by a single event.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor commissions the Siku Quanshu-precursor cataloguing projects
events.description: The Qianlong Emperor's reign (1735–1796) was at its zenith in 1752, with the Qing Empire enjoying internal stability and prosperity. The emperor continued his extensive patronage of arts, literature, and scholarly projects, commissioning works that would lay groundwork for the later Siku Quanshu (Complete Library of the Four Treasuries) project. The court also maintained the system of literary inquisition (wenziyu), suppressing texts deemed subversive to Manchu rule. The year fell within the broader 'High Qing' period of demographic expansion, commercial growth, and territorial consolidation.
key_figures: Qianlong Emperor (Aisin Gioro Hongli, r. 1735–1796)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty), court records for the Qianlong reign
Mark C. Elliott, Emperor Qianlong: Son of Heaven, Man of the World (2009)

events.certainty_note: General conditions of the Qianlong court in 1752 are well documented in the Veritable Records and other Qing sources; specific cataloguing projects are attested but the Siku Quanshu itself was not formally commissioned until 1773.

events.title: Dalibard conducts the sentry-box experiment in France, confirming Franklin's lightning hypothesis
events.description: On 10 May 1752, Thomas-François Dalibard successfully conducted the sentry-box experiment at Marly-la-Ville near Paris, following the design proposed by Benjamin Franklin. A forty-foot iron rod was erected on an insulated platform; during a thunderstorm, sparks were drawn from the rod, proving that thunderclouds carry electrical charge. This was the first experimental verification of Franklin's hypothesis and preceded Franklin's own kite experiment by approximately a month. The success was reported to the French Académie des Sciences and quickly spread across Europe, establishing Franklin's international reputation.
key_figures: Thomas-François Dalibard (French naturalist)
Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon (patron who supported the experiment)
Benjamin Franklin (originator of the hypothesis)

sources.name: Thomas-François Dalibard, report to the Académie Royale des Sciences (13 May 1752)
J.L. Heilbron, Electricity in the 17th and 18th Centuries (1979)

events.certainty_note: Dalibard's report to the Académie survives and the experiment was witnessed by multiple observers.

events.title: Maratha expansion continues across central and southern India
events.description: The Maratha Confederacy under Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao (Nanasaheb) continued to expand its influence across the Indian subcontinent in 1752. Maratha armies operated across central India, the Deccan, and increasingly projected power northward toward Delhi and the Mughal heartland, filling the vacuum left by declining Mughal authority. The Peshwa's government in Pune directed a loose confederacy of powerful chiefs — the Scindias, Holkars, Bhonsles, and Gaekwads — who controlled vast territories. This expansion set the stage for eventual Maratha-Durrani conflict in northern India.
key_figures: Balaji Baji Rao / Nanasaheb (Peshwa of the Maratha Empire)
Raghunath Rao (Peshwa's brother, military commander)

sources.name: Marathi-language correspondence (Peshwa Daftar, records preserved at the Deccan College, Pune)
Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600–1818 (The New Cambridge History of India, 1993)

events.certainty_note: Maratha administrative records (the Peshwa Daftar) provide detailed contemporary documentation.

events.title: Tupac Amaru I's descendants maintain indigenous resistance traditions in Peru
events.description: In the Viceroyalty of Peru, the mid-18th century saw continuing tensions between Spanish colonial authorities and indigenous communities, particularly in the southern Andes around Cusco. The descendants of Inca nobility maintained their social status and cultural identity under colonial rule, and local indigenous leaders (curacas/kurakas) navigated an increasingly burdensome system of forced labor (mita) and taxation. While no major rebellion occurred in 1752, the structural conditions — including the Bourbon reforms' tightening of colonial extraction — were building toward the massive Tupac Amaru II rebellion of 1780.
key_figures: José Gabriel Condorcanqui (future Tupac Amaru II, born 1738, active as a curaca in this period's broader context)

sources.name: Spanish colonial administrative records (Archivo General de Indias)
Charles Walker, The Tupac Amaru Rebellion (2014)

events.certainty_note: General conditions are well documented in colonial archives; this entry reflects structural context rather than a specific discrete event in 1752.

events.title: Asante Empire consolidates power in West Africa under Opoku Ware I
events.description: The Asante Empire under Asantehene Opoku Ware I (r. c. 1720–1750) had recently reached its greatest territorial extent, controlling much of modern Ghana and dominating the gold and slave trades of the Gold Coast hinterland. Opoku Ware I died around 1750, and his successor Kusi Obodom (r. c. 1750–1764) faced the challenge of maintaining this vast territorial state. The Asante continued to manage complex diplomatic and commercial relationships with European trading forts on the coast (Dutch, British, Danish) and with neighboring African states.
key_figures: Kusi Obodom (Asantehene, r. c. 1750–1764)

sources.name: Dutch West India Company records from Elmina; oral traditions collected by later scholars
Ivor Wilks, Asante in the Nineteenth Century (1975; relevant for earlier context chapters)

events.certainty_note: Asante chronology for this period relies heavily on oral tradition correlated with European trading post records. Exact regnal dates are approximate (±1–2 years).

disconfirming_evidence: Franklin's kite experiment (1752-002) is one of the most disputed events of this year. Historian Tom Tucker (Bolt of Fate, 2003) argued that the experiment may never have occurred as described, noting that Franklin's account was published months after the alleged event, no witnesses are named, and the experiment as described would likely have been lethal. While most historians accept that Franklin conducted some version of the experiment, the precise details remain contested. The 'Give us our eleven days' riots supposedly provoked by the calendar change (1752-001) are largely a myth: the claim that mobs rioted against the lost days derives primarily from a satirical William Hogarth painting ('An Election Entertainment', 1755) and was amplified by later antiquarian writers. Robert Poole's research (1998) found no evidence of significant popular unrest specifically over the calendar change itself, though there were unrelated election disturbances. For the Asante succession (1752-020), the dates of Opoku Ware I's death and Kusi Obodom's accession vary by 1–3 years depending on the source tradition consulted.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1752 is rich for Europe and the European colonial world, with extensive parliamentary records, diplomatic correspondence, newspapers, and personal papers surviving. Coverage of South and Southeast Asia relies on a mix of indigenous court chronicles (often compiled later) and European trading company records, which introduce commercial and colonial biases. The Persian civil wars of this period are documented in chronicles written under later dynasties with their own political agendas. Sub-Saharan African history depends heavily on European coastal trading records and oral traditions collected in the 19th–20th centuries, leaving interior developments poorly attested. Chinese records are extensive but access and translation into Western languages remain uneven. The calendar transition makes precise dating of British events in September 1752 sometimes confusing in secondary sources.

graph_edges.note: The Charleston hurricane struck just days after the calendar change took effect, creating dating confusion in some sources
Franklin's kite experiment and Dalibard's sentry-box experiment were both tests of Franklin's lightning hypothesis, with Dalibard's preceding Franklin's by approximately one month
The Mon capture of Ava directly provoked Alaungpaya's resistance movement
Durrani expansion into Punjab and Maratha expansion in central India were converging trajectories that would collide at the Third Battle of Panipat (1761)
French-British rivalry in the Ohio Valley was a direct precursor to the outbreak of the French and Indian War / Seven Years' War
The normalization of Bourbon-Habsburg relations at Aranjuez prefigured the full Diplomatic Revolution alliance of 1756
Hume's Political Discourses directly influenced Adam Smith and the development of classical economics

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
